documentation: This project aims to provide numerical algorithms for ultrashort laser pulse measurement methods

modules.name: pypret.io.io
documentation:
A module that provides mixin classes to enable persistence with HDF5 files.

Author: Nils Geib, nils.geib@uni-jena.de

functions.name: pypret.io.io.save
documentation:
Saves an object in an HDF5 file.

# Parameters

val

: Any Python value that is made up of storeable instances. Those are
  built-in types, numpy datatypes and types with custom handlers.

path

: Save path of the HDF5 file. Existing files will be overwritten!

archive

: If `True` will compress the whole hdf5 file. This is useful when
  dealing with (many) small HDF5 files as those contain significant
  overhead.

options

: The HDF5 options that will be used for saving. Defaults to the
  global options instance `DEFAULT_OPTIONS`.

functions.name: pypret.io.io._savez
documentation: Saves an object in a compressed HDF5 file.

functions.name: pypret.io.io._save
documentation:
Saves an object in an HDF5 file.

Parameters
----------
val : object
    Any Python value that is made up of storeable instances. Those are
    built-in types, numpy datatypes and types with custom handlers.
path : str or Path instance
    Save path of the HDF5 file. Existing files will be overwritten!
options : HDF5Options instance
    The HDF5 options that will be used for saving. Defaults to the
    global options instance `DEFAULT_OPTIONS`.

functions.name: pypret.io.io.load
documentation:
Reads a possibly compressed HDF5 file.

If archive is `None` it is retrieved with python-magic.

class_variables.name: pypret.io.io.IOHandler.instances
documentation: instances are filled by MetaIO

classes.name: pypret.io.io.MetaIO
documentation:
All sub-classes of IO are automatically registered with the default
IOHandler. This allows easy storage by simply subclassing IO.

classes.name: pypret.io.io.IO

methods.name: pypret.io.io.IO._post_init
documentation: Hook to initialize an object from storage.

Provides an interface for saving to and loading from a HDF5 file.

This class can be mixed-in to easily add persistence to your existing
Python classes. By default all attributes of an object will be stored.
Upon loading these attributes will be loaded and `__init__` will *not*
be called.

Often a better way is to store only the necessary attributes by
giving a list of attribute names in the private attribute `_io_store`.
Then you have to overwrite the `_post_init()` method that initializes
your object from these stored attributes. It is usually also be called at
the end of the original `__init__` and should not mean extra effort.

Lastly, you can simply overwrite `load_from_dict` to implement a
completely custom loader.

modules.name: pypret.io
documentation:
A subpackage that provides Python object persistence in HDF5 files.

It was written to make the storage of arbitrary nested Python structures
in the exchangable HDF5 format easy. Its main purpose is to easily add
persistence to existing numerical or data analysis codes.

While the files itself are plain HDF5 and can be read in any language
supporting HDF5, the format is not compatible to Matlab's own file format.
If you are searching for such a solution look at the hdf5storage package.

# Usage

The module exports a `save()` function that stores arbitrary structures
of Python and NumPy data types. For example

```pycon
>>> x = {'data': [1, 2, 3], 'xrange': np.arange(5, dtype=np.uint8)}
>>> io.save(x, "test.hdf5")
```

This function should suffice for most needs as long as only standard types
are used. The `load()` function loads these files and restores the structure
and the types of the data:

```pycon
>>> io.load("test.hdf5")
{'data': [1, 2, 3], 'xrange': array([0, 1, 2, 3, 4], dtype=uint8)}
```

# Custom Objects

If you are using objects as simple containers without functionality you may
consider using the SimpleNamespace class from the `types` module of the
standard library. The advantage is that io knows how to handle it.:

```
from types import SimpleNamespace
a = SimpleNamespace(name="my object", data=np.arange(5))
a.data2 = np.arange(10)
copra.save(a)
```

If your objects are containers with methods but without a custom `__init__()`
the simplest way is to inherit or mix-in the `IO` class:

```
class Data(io.IO):
    x = 1

    def squared(self):
        return self.x * self.x
```

When using the `IO` class by default all instance attributes are stored
and loaded. More flexibility can be achieved by specifying `_io`-attributes
of your custom class.

> \_io_store
>
> : Specify the the instance attributes that are stored exclusively. Acts
>   as a whitelist. If `None` all instance attributes are stored. Default
>   is `None`.
>
> \_io_store_not
>
> : Specify which instance attributes are not stored. Acts as a blacklist.
>   If `None` no blacklisting is done.

If you want to add attributes to storage you can call the
`_io_add_to_storage(key)` method on your instance.
The IO class initalizes the instance without calling `__init__()`. Instead
`__new__()` is called on the class and afterwards the `_post_init()`
method which subclasses can implement. A fully working example of a class
is the following (reduced from copra.FourierTransform):

```
class Grid(io.IO):
    _io_store = ['N', 'dx', 'x0']

    def __init__(self, N, dx, x0=0.0):
        # This is _not_ called upon loading from storage
        self.N = N
        self.dx = dx
        self.x0 = x0
        self._post_init()

    def _post_init(self):
        # this is called upon loading from storage
        # calculate the grids
        n = np.arange(self.N)
        self.x = self.x0 + n * self.dx
```

In this example the object can be exactly reproduced upon loading but only
a minimal amount of storage is required.

If you want to implement your own storage interface for a custom object
you should inherit from `IO` and implement your own `to_dict()` and
`from_dict()` methods. Look at the implementation of the default in `IO`
to understand their behavior.

# File Format

The file format this module uses is a straightforward mapping of Python
types to the HDF5 data structure. Dictionaries and objects are mapped to
HDF5 groups, numpy arrays use h5py's type translation.
Iterables are converted to groups by introducing artificial keys of the
type `idx_%d`. This is rather inefficient which explains why the
module should not be used to store large numerical arrays as a Python list.
To store the type information it uses an HDF5 attribute `__class__`.
Furthermore, for scalars the attribute `__dtype__` and for strings the
attribute `__encoding__` are additionally used.

In conclusion, nested structures of Python types stored with this package are
not suitable for exchanging. Dictionaries of numerical data stored with this
package can be easily opened with any program that supports HDF5.

modules.name: pypret.io.options
documentation:
Sets the default options for HDF5 data.

Author: Nils Geib, nils.geib@uni-jena.de

classes.name: pypret.io.options.HDF5Options

methods.name: pypret.io.options.HDF5Options.__call__
documentation: Returns the correct dataset creation options for an array.

A class that handles the correct HDF5 options for different data sets.

The reason is simply that native HDF5 compression will actually increase
the file size for small arrays (< 300 bytes). This class selects different
HDF5 options based on the dataset over the method `__call__`. It can
be subclassed to support more sophisticated selection strategies.

modules.name: pypret.io.handlers
documentation:
Implements functions that handle the serialization of types and classes.

Type handlers store and load objects of exactly that type. Instance handlers
work also work for subclasses of that type.

The instance handlers are processed in the order they are stored. This means
that if an object is an instance of several handled classes it will not raise
an error and will be handled by the first matching handler in the OrderedDict.

functions.name: pypret.io.handlers.classname
documentation:
Returns a qualified class name as string.

The qualified class name consists of the module and the class name,
separated by a dot. If an instance is passed to this function, the name
of its class is returned.

Parameters
----------
val : instance or class
    The instance or a class of which the qualified class name is returned.

Returns
-------
str : The qualified class name.

functions.name: pypret.io.handlers.save_to_level
documentation: A generic save function that dispatches the correct handler.

functions.name: pypret.io.handlers.load_from_level
documentation:
Loads an object from an HDF5 group or dataset.

Parameters
----------
level : h5py.Dataset or h5py.Group
    An HDF5 node that stores an object in a valid format.
obj : instance or None
    If provided this instance will be updated from the HDF5 node instead
    of creating a new instance of the stored object.

Returns
-------
instance of the stored object

classes.name: pypret.io.handlers.TypeRegister
documentation: Metaclass that registers a type handler in a global dictionary.

classes.name: pypret.io.handlers.InstanceRegister
documentation: Metaclass that registers an instance handler in global dictionary.

methods.name: pypret.io.handlers.Handler.save_to_level
documentation:
A generic wrapper around the custom save method that each
handler implements. It creates a dataset or a group depending
on the `level_type` class attribute and sets the `__class__`
attribute correctly.
For more flexibility subclasses can overwrite this method.

methods.name: pypret.io.handlers.Handler.load_from_level
documentation: The loader that has to be implemented by subclasses.

classes.name: pypret.io.handlers.TypeHandler
documentation: Handles data of a specific type or class.

classes.name: pypret.io.handlers.InstanceHandler
documentation:
Handles all instances of a specific (parent) class.

If an instance is subclass to several classes for which a handler exists,
no error will be raised (in contrast to TypeHandler). Rather, the first
match in the global instance_saver_handlers OrderedDict will be used.

classes.name: pypret.io.handlers.NoneHandler
documentation: Specific handlers

classes.name: pypret.io.handlers.IntHandler
documentation:
Special int handler to deal with Python's variable size ints.

They are stored as byte arrays. Probably not the most efficient solution...

classes.name: pypret.io.handlers.ListHandler
documentation: Despite its name it also handles tuples.

modules.name: pypret.pulse_error
documentation: This module implements testing procedures for retrieval algorithms.

functions.name: pypret.pulse_error.pulse_error
documentation:
Calculates the normalized rms error between two pulse spectra while
taking into account the retrieval ambiguities.

One step in `optimal_rms_error` (the determination of the initial bracket)
could probably be more efficient, see [^cite_dorrer2002]). We use the less
elegant but maybe more straightforward way of simply sampling the
range for a bracket that encloses a minimum.

# Parameters

E, E0: 1d-array

: Complex-valued arrays that contain the spectra of the pulses.
  `E` will be matched against `E0`.

ft

: Performs Fourier transforms on the pulse grid.

dot_ambiguity

: Takes the direction of time ambiguity into account. Default is
  `False`.

spectral_shift_ambiguity

: Takes the spectral shift ambiguity into account. Default is `False`.

functions.name: pypret.pulse_error.best_constant_phase
documentation:
Finds `c` with `|c| = 1` so that `sum(abs2(c * y1 - y2))` is
minimal.

Uses an analytic solution.

functions.name: pypret.pulse_error.optimal_rms_error
documentation:
Calculates the RMS error of two arrays, ignoring scaling, constant
and linear phase of one of them.

Formally it calculates the minimal error:

```
R = sqrt(|rho * exp(i*(x*a + b)) * y1 - y2|^2 / |y2|^2)
```

with respect to rho, a and b. If additionally `conjugation = True` then
the error for conjugate(y1) is calculated and the best transformation of y1
is also returned.

modules.name: pypret.pulse
documentation:
Provides a class to simulate an ultrashort optical pulse using its envelope
description.

The temporal envelope is denoted as `field` and the spectral envelope as
`spectrum` in the code and the function signatures.

classes.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.__init__
documentation:
Initializes an optical pulse described by its envelope.

# Parameters

ft

: A `FourierTransform` instance that specifies a temporal and
  spectral grid.

wl0

: The center frequency of the pulse.

unit

: The unit in which the center frequency is specified. Can be either
  of `wl`, `om`, `f`, or `k`. See `frequencies` for more
  information. Default is `wl`.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.copy
documentation:
Returns a copy of the pulse object.

Note that they still reference the same `FourierTransform` instance,
which is assumed to be immutable.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.field
documentation:
The complex-valued temporal envelope of the pulse.

On read access returns a copy of the internal array. On write
access the spectral envelope is automatically updated.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.field_at
documentation:
The complex-valued temporal envelope of the pulse at the
times `t`.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.spectrum
documentation:
The complex-valued spectral envelope of the pulse.

On read access returns a copy of the internal array. On write
access the temporal envelope is automatically updated.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.spectrum_at
documentation:
The complex-valued spectral envelope of the pulse at the
frequencies `w`.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.update_field
documentation: Manually updates the field from the (modified) spectrum.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.update_spectrum
documentation: Manually updates the spectrum from the (modified) field.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.intensity
documentation:
The temporal intensity profile of the pulse in vacuum.

Only read access.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.amplitude
documentation:
The temporal amplitude profile of the pulse in vacuum.

Only read access.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.phase
documentation:
The temporal phase of the pulse.

Only read access.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.spectral_intensity
documentation:
The spectral intensity profile of the pulse in vacuum.

Only read access.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.spectral_amplitude
documentation:
The spectral amplitude profile of the pulse in vacuum.

Only read access.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.spectral_phase
documentation:
The spectral phase of the pulse.

Only read access.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.time_bandwidth_product
documentation:
Calculates the rms time-bandwidth product of the pulse.

In this definition a transform-limited Gaussian pulse has a
time-bandwidth product of 0.5. So the number returned by this
function will always be >= 0.5.

methods.name: pypret.pulse.Pulse.fwhm
documentation:
Calculates the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the temporal
intensity profile.

Parameters
----------
dt : float or None, optional
    Specifies the required accuracy of the calculation. If `None` (the
    default) it is only as good as the spacing of the underlying
    simulation grid - which can be quite coarse compared to the FWHM.
    If smaller it is calculated based on trigonometric interpolation.
A class for modelling femtosecond pulses by their envelope.

modules.name: pypret.graphics
documentation: This module implements several helper routines for plotting.

modules.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever
documentation: This module provides the basic classes for the pulse retrieval algorithms.

functions.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.Retriever
documentation:
Creates a retriever instance.

# Parameters

pnps

: A PNPS instance that is used to simulate a PNPS measurement.

method

: Type of solver. Should be one of
  : - 'copra' {class}`(see here) <COPRARetriever>`
    - 'gpa' {class}`(see here) <GPARetriever>`
    - 'gp-dscan' {class}`(see here) <GPDSCANRetriever>`
    - 'pcgpa' {class}`(see here) <PCGPARetriever>`
    - 'pie' {class}`(see here) <PIERetriever>`
    - 'lm' {class}`(see here) <LMRetriever>`
    - 'bfgs' {class}`(see here) <BFGSRetriever>`
    - 'de' {class}`(see here) <DERetriever>`
    - 'nelder-mead' {class}`(see here) <NMRetriever>`

  'copra' is the default choice.

maxiter

: The maximum number of algorithm iterations. The default is 300.

maxfev

: The maximum number of function evaluations. If given, the algorithms
  stop before this number is reached. Not all algorithms support this
  feature. Default is `None`, in which case it is ignored.

logging

: Stores trace errors and pulses over the iterations if supported
  by the retriever class. Default is `False`.

verbose

: Prints out trace errors during the iteration if supported by the
  retriever class. Default is `False`.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.MetaRetriever
documentation: Metaclass that registers Retriever classes in a global dictionary.
Metaclass and factory

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever.retrieve
documentation:
Retrieve pulse from `measurement` starting at `initial_guess`.

# Parameters

measurement

: A MeshData instance that contains the PNPS measurement. The first
  axis has to correspond to the PNPS parameter, the second to the
  frequency. The data has to be the measured \_intensity\_ over the
  frequency (not wavelength!). The second axis has to match exactly
  the frequency axis of the underlying PNPS instance. No
  interpolation is done.

initial_guess

: The spectrum of the pulse that is used as initial guess in the
  iterative retrieval.

weights

: Weights that are attributed to the measurement for retrieval.
  In the case of (assumed) Gaussian uncertainties with standard
  deviation sigma they should correspond to 1/sigma.
  Not all algorithms support using the weights.

kwargs

: Can override retrieval options specified in {func}`__init__`.

# Notes

This function provides no interpolation or data processing. You have
to write a retriever wrapper for that purpose.

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever._project
documentation: Performs the projection on the measured intensity.

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever._objective_function
documentation:
Calculates the minimization objective from the pulse spectrum.

This is Eq. 11 in the paper:

    r = sum (Tmn^meas - mu * Tmn)

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever.trace_error
documentation: Calculates the trace error from the pulse spectrum.

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever._r
documentation: Calculates the minimization objective r from a simulated trace Tmn.

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever._error_vector
documentation:
Calculates the residual vector from measured to simulated
intensity.

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever._R
documentation: Calculates the trace error from a simulated trace Tmn.

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever._Rr
documentation: Calculates the trace error from the minimization objective r.

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.retriever.BaseRetriever.result
documentation:
Analyzes the retrieval results in one retrieval instance
and processes it for plotting or storage.

The abstract base class for pulse retrieval.

This class implements common functionality for different retrieval
algorithms.
Retriever Base class

modules.name: pypret.retrieval
documentation:
This sub-package implements different pulse retrieval algorithms
using a common interface. This facilitates direct comparison.

All algorithms are implemented as a subclass of {class}`BaseRetriever`. The
algorithms which are implemented step-by-step, i.e., do not rely on some
monolithic minimization algorithm implemented elsewhere, are further
subclassed from {class}`StepRetriever`.

The public function to instantiate the correct retriever is {func}`Retriever`.

This sub-package does not implement any form of data pre-processing. It expects
correctly interpolated data in form of a MeshData object.

modules.name: pypret.pnps
documentation:
This module provides classes to calculate parametrized nonlinear process
spectra (PNPS), such as frequency-resolved optical gating (FROG),
interferometric FROG (iFROG), dispersion scan (d-scan), time-domain
ptychography (TDP) and pulse-shaper assisted methods such as multiphoton
intrapulse interference phase scan (MIIPS).

The code follows the notation used in [^cite_geib2019] and its supplement.

Currently only the abovementioned methods are implemented. But the code is
written in such way that including new pulse measurement methods is very easy.
If it is a method using a collinear nonlinearity, subclass from
`CollinearPNPS`, otherwise from `NoncollinearPNPS`.

In the collinear case only `self.mask(parameter)` has to be implemented which
calculates the used linear parametrization operator. In the non-collinear
case the function `_calculate` has to be implemented which calculates and
returns the PNPS trace `T_mn` and the PNPS signal `S_mk`.

functions.name: pypret.pnps.PNPS
documentation:
Creates a PNPS instance.

# Parameters

pulse

: A pulse instance that is used to simulate the PNPS trace.

method
: The type of PNPS measurement. Should be one of
  : - 'frog' {class}`(see here) <pypret.pnps.FROG>`
    - 'tdp' {class}`(see here) <pypret.pnps.TDP>`
    - 'dscan' {class}`(see here) <pypret.pnps.DSCAN>`
    - 'miips' {class}`(see here) <pypret.pnps.MIIPS>`
    - 'ifrog' {class}`(see here) <pypret.pnps.IFROG>`

process

: The nonlinear process used in the measurement method. Can be one of
  : - 'shg' : second harmonic generation
    - 'thg' : third harmonic generation
    - 'sd' : self-diffraction
    - 'pg' : polarization gating

  Not all methods support all nonlinear processes. In that case a
  ValueError will be raised.

Additional parameters are described in the documentation of the specific
PNPS methods.
Factory method

classes.name: pypret.pnps.MetaPNPS
documentation: Metaclass that registers PNPS classes in a global dictionary.
Metaclass and factory

classes.name: pypret.pnps.BasePNPS

methods.name: pypret.pnps.BasePNPS.measure
documentation:
Simulates the measurement process.

Note that we deal with the spectrum over the frequency!
For retrieving from actual data we need to rescale this by lambda^2.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.BasePNPS.calculate
documentation:
Calculates the PNPS signal S_mk and the PNPS trace T_mn.

# Parameters

spectrum

: The pulse spectrum for which the PNPS trace is calculated.

parameter

: The PNPS parameter (array) for which the PNPS trace is calculated.

# Returns

1d- or 2d-array

: Returns the calculated PNPS trace over the frequency
  `self.process_w`. If parameter was a scalar a 1d-array is
  returned. If it was a 1d-array a 2d-array is returned where the
  parameter runs along the first axis and the frequency along the
  second.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.BasePNPS.intermediate
documentation: Returns intermediate results as stored by the instance.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.BasePNPS.gradient
documentation: Calculates the gradient ∇_n Z_m.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.BasePNPS.trace
documentation: Returns the last calculated trace as a MeshData object.
The PNPS base class
PNPS Base class

classes.name: pypret.pnps.CollinearPNPS

methods.name: pypret.pnps.CollinearPNPS._calculate
documentation:
Calculates the nonlinear process spectrum for a single parameter.

Follows the notation from our paper.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.CollinearPNPS._gradient
documentation: Returns the gradient of Z based on the previous call to _spectrum.
Implements collinear methods: d-scan, iFROG, etc.
Collinear PNPS methods

classes.name: pypret.pnps.MIIPS

methods.name: pypret.pnps.MIIPS.__init__
documentation:
Creates the instance.

Parameters
----------
pulse : Pulse instance
    The pulse object that defines the simulation grid.
process : str
    The nonlinear process used in the PNPS method.
alpha : float
    The amplitude of the phase pattern (in rad).
gamma : float
    The frequency of the phase pattern in Hz.

Implements the multiphoton intrapulse interference phase scan method
(MIIPS) [Lozovoy2004]_ [Xu2006]_.

classes.name: pypret.pnps.IFROG

methods.name: pypret.pnps.IFROG.__init__
documentation:
Creates the instance.

Parameters
----------
pulse : Pulse instance
    The pulse object that defines the simulation grid.
process : str
    The nonlinear process used in the PNPS method.

Implements the interferometric frequency-resolved optical gating
method [^footnote-1].

[^footnote-1]: G. Stibenz and G. Steinmeyer, "Interferometric frequency-resolved
    optical gating," Opt. Express 13, 2617-2626 (OSA, 2005).

classes.name: pypret.pnps.DSCAN
documentation:
Implements the dispersion scan method [Miranda2012a]_ [Miranda2012b]_.

Not implemented in the public version of the code. Please contact us
if you want to use pypret for d-scan measurements.

classes.name: pypret.pnps.NoncollinearPNPS
documentation: Implements non-collinear methods: FROG, TDP, etc.
Noncollinear PNPS methods

classes.name: pypret.pnps.FROG

methods.name: pypret.pnps.FROG.__init__
documentation:
Creates the instance.

Parameters
----------
pulse : Pulse instance
    The pulse object that defines the simulation grid.
process : str
    The nonlinear process used in the PNPS method.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.FROG._calculate
documentation:
Calculates the nonlinear process spectrum for a single parameter.

Follows the notation from our paper.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.FROG._gradient
documentation: Returns the gradient of Z based on the previous call to _spectrum.

Implements frequency-resolved optical gating [Kane1993]_
[Trebino2000]_.

classes.name: pypret.pnps.TDP

methods.name: pypret.pnps.TDP.__init__
documentation:
Creates the instance.

Parameters
----------
pulse : Pulse instance
    The pulse object that defines the simulation grid.
process : str
    The nonlinear process used in the PNPS method.
center : float
    The center wavelength of the bandwidth filter in m.
width : float
    The width (FWHM) of the bandwidth filter in m.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.TDP._calculate
documentation:
Calculates the nonlinear process spectrum for a single parameter.

Follows the notation from our paper.

methods.name: pypret.pnps.TDP._gradient
documentation: Returns the gradient of Z based on the previous call to _spectrum.

Implements a variant of time-domain ptychography. This version is
self-referenced and works like FROG except that in one arm of the
correlator the bandwidth of the pulse is heavily filtered [Witting2016]_.
Other variants are not directly supported by this class.

modules.name: pypret.retrieval.nlo_retriever
documentation:
This module implements retrieval algorithms based on general
nonlinear optimization algorithms such as Levenberg-Marquadt,
differential evolution, or Nelder-Mead.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.nlo_retriever.LMRetriever

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.nlo_retriever.LMRetriever.__init__
documentation:
For a full documentation of the arguments see {class}`Retriever`.

For the documentation of `ftol`, `xtol`, `gtol` see the documentation
of {func}`scipy.optimize.least_squares`. They are passed directly
to the optimizer. If you want to run the optimizer for a fixed
number of iterations, set all values to 1e-14 to effectively
disable the stopping criteria.

Implements pulse retrieval based on the Levenberg-Marquadt algorithm.

This is an efficient nonlinear least-squares solver, however, it will still
be *very* slow for large pulses (N > 256). The reason is that the
(MN x N) Jacobian is evaluated using numerical differentiation.

The recommendation is to use this method either on small problems or to
refine or verify solutions provided by a different algorithm.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.nlo_retriever.DERetriever
documentation:
This retriever uses the gradient-free differential evolution algorithm.

It tries to match the parameters described in [^cite_escoto2018] as far as
they are mentioned. No further effort was made to optimize them. If you
are interested in using DE as a pulse retrieval algorithm you are
advised to study the documentation at
{func}`scipy.optimize.differential_evolution`.

The initial population in our implementation is based on the provided guess
with added complex, Gaussian noise of 5% of the maximum amplitude.
In our tests we saw no convergence when starting from completely
random initial guesses.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.nlo_retriever.NMRetriever
documentation: This retriever uses the gradient-free Nelder-Mead algorithm.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.nlo_retriever.BFGSRetriever
documentation: This retriever uses the BFGS algorithm with numerical differentiation.

modules.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever
documentation:
This module implements specific pulse retrieval algorithms, e.g.,
COPRA, GPA, PCGPA, etc.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.COPRARetriever

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.COPRARetriever.__init__
documentation:
For a full documentation of the arguments see {class}`Retriever`.

# Parameters

alpha

: Scales the step size in the global stage of COPRA. Higher values
  mean potentially faster convergence but less accuracy. Lower
  values provide higher accuracy for the cost of speed. Default is
  0.25.

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.COPRARetriever._retrieve_step
documentation:
Perform a single COPRA step.

Parameters
----------
iteration : int
    The current iteration number - mainly for logging.
En : 1d-array
    The current pulse spectrum.

This module implements the common pulse retrieval algorithm
[Geib2019]_.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.PCGPARetriever

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.PCGPARetriever.__init__
documentation:
For a full documentation of the arguments see {class}`Retriever`.

# Parameters

decomposition

: It specifies how the FROG signal is decomposed. If `power` (the
  default) the power method is used to find the largest eigenvalue.
  If `svd` a full singular value decomposition is performed. This
  is potentially much slower but more accurate.

This class implements the principal components generalized projections
algorithm (PCGPA) for SHG-FROG.

We follow the algorithm as described in [Kane1999]_ but use the PNPS
formalism from [Geib2019]_ and some minor modifications:

- it supports both the singular value decomposition and the power
  method to find/approximate the largest eigenvector.
- the projection includes the scaling factor µ. This makes the method
  robust against initial guesses with the wrong magnitude. It should
  have no adverse effect.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.GPARetriever

methods.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.GPARetriever.__init__
documentation:
For a full documentation of the arguments see {class}`Retriever`.

# Parameters

step_size

: Specifies how the step size of the gradient step in GPA is
  determined. Default is `exact` which performs an exact line search.
  `inexact` performs a backtracking line search and `copra` uses the
  ad-hoc estimates for the step size used in COPRA.

Implements the classical generalized projections algorithm for
SHG-FROG as described in [DeLong1994]_ and [Trebino2000].

As far as I know the determination of the step size in GPA is not
made explicit in the publications. It is usually done in a line search.
In this implementation we offer three different options:

- an exact line search using a Brent style minimizer
- a backtracking (inexact) line search using the Armijo-Goldstein
  condition with c=0.5 and tau=0.5.
- the same heuristic choice for the step size used in copra.

The last method is the fastest, but as the first is the classic choice
for GPA, it is the default.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.GPDSCANRetriever
documentation:
This class implements a pulse retrieval algorithm for SHG and THG
d-scan based on the paper [Miranda2017]_.

In our tests we found that it does not converge in the noiseless case.
In other words the global solution to the least-squares problem is not a
fixed point of the iteration.

classes.name: pypret.retrieval.step_retriever.PIERetriever
documentation:
This class implements a pulse retrieval algorithm for SHG-FROG based on
the ptychographical iterative engine (PIE). It is based on the paper
[Sidorenko2016]_ and its erratum [Sidorenko2017]_.

We modified the algorithm to include the scaling factor µ in the
projection. This makes the method robust against initial guesses with the
wrong magnitude. It should have no adverse effect.

modules.name: pypret.material
documentation:
This module provides classes to calculate the refractive index
based on Sellmeier equations.

This is required to correctly model d-scan measurements.

Currently only very few materials are implemented. But more should be easy
to add. If the refractive index is described by formula 1 or 2 from
refractiveindex.info you can simply instantiate `SellmeierF1` or `SellmeierF2`.
If not, inherit from BaseMaterial and implement the `self._func` method.

variables.name: pypret.material.FS
documentation:
Material instance describing fused silica (fused quartz).

The data was taken from refractiveindex.info

variables.name: pypret.material.BK7
documentation:
Material instance describing N-BK7 (SCHOTT).

The data was taken from refractiveindex.info

classes.name: pypret.material.BaseMaterial

methods.name: pypret.material.BaseMaterial.__init__
documentation:
Creates a dispersive material.

Parameters
----------
coefficients: ndarray
    The Sellmeier coefficients.
freq_range : iterable
    The wavelength range in which the Sellmeier equation is valid
    (given in m).
check_bounds : bool, optional
    Specifies if the frequency argument should be checked on every
    evaluation to match the allowed range.
scaling : float, optional
    Specifies the scaling of the Sellmeier formula. E.g., most
    Sellmeier formulas are defined in terms of µm (micrometer), 
    whereas our function interface works in meter. In that case the
    scaling would be `1e6`. Default is `1.0e6`.

methods.name: pypret.material.BaseMaterial._convert
documentation: This is intended for conversion to be used in `self._func`.

methods.name: pypret.material.BaseMaterial.n
documentation: The refractive index at frequency `x` specified in units `unit`.

methods.name: pypret.material.BaseMaterial.k
documentation: The wavenumber in the material in rad / m.
Abstract base class for dispersive materials.

classes.name: pypret.material.SellmeierF1
documentation:
Defines a dispersive material via a specific Sellmeier equation.

This subclass supports materials with a Sellmeier equation of the
form:

```
n^2(l) - 1 = c1 + c2 * l^2 / (l2 - c3^2) + ...
```

This is formula 1 from refractiveindex.info [^cite_dispersionformulas].

classes.name: pypret.material.SellmeierF2
documentation:
Defines a dispersive material via a specific Sellmeier equation.

This subclass supports materials with a Sellmeier equation of the
form:

```
n^2(l) - 1 = c1 + c2 * l^2 / (l2 - c3) + ...
```

This is formula 2 from refractiveindex.info [^cite_dispersionformulas].

modules.name: pypret.mesh_data
documentation: This module implements an object for dealing with two-dimensional data.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.__init__
documentation:
Creates a MeshData instance.

# Parameters

data

: A at least two-dimensional array containing the data.

axes

: Arrays specifying the coordinates of the data axes. Must be given
  in indexing order.

uncertainty

: An ndarray of the same size as `data` that contains some measure
  of the uncertainty of the meshdata. E.g., it could be the standard
  deviation of the data.

labels

: A list of strings labeling the axes. The last element labels the
  data itself, e.g. `labels` must have one more element than the
  number of axes.

units

: A list of unit strings.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.shape
documentation: Returns the shape of the data as a tuple.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.ndim
documentation: Returns the dimension of the data as integer.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.copy
documentation: Creates a copy of the MeshData instance.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.marginals
documentation:
Calculates the marginals of the data.

axes specifies the axes of the marginals, e.g., the axes on which the
sum is projected.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.normalize
documentation: Normalizes the maximum of the data to 1.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.autolimit
documentation: Limits the data based on the marginals.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.limit
documentation:
Limits the data range of this instance.

Parameters
----------
*limits : tuples
    The data limits in the axes as tuples. Has to match the dimension
    of the data or the number of axes specified in the `axes`
    parameter.
axes : tuple or None
    The axes in which the limit is applied. Default is `None` in which
    case all axes are selected.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.interpolate
documentation:
Interpolates the data on a new two-dimensional, equidistantly
spaced grid.

methods.name: pypret.mesh_data.MeshData.flip
documentation: Flips the data on the specified axes.

modules.name: pypret
documentation:
Disclaimer
----------

THIS CODE IS FOR EDUCATIONAL PURPOSES ONLY! The code in this package was not
optimized for accuracy or performance. Rather it aims to provide a simple
implementation of the basic algorithms.

Author: Nils C. Geib, nils.geib@uni-jena.de

modules.name: pypret.lib
documentation:
Miscellaneous helper functions

These functions fulfill small numerical tasks used in several places in the
package.

functions.name: pypret.lib.as_list
documentation:
Try to convert argument to list and return it.

Useful to implement function arguments that could be scalar values
or lists.

functions.name: pypret.lib.abs2
documentation: Calculates the squared magnitude of a complex array.

functions.name: pypret.lib.rms
documentation: Calculates the root mean square (rms) error between `x` and `y`.

functions.name: pypret.lib.norm2
documentation: Calculates the squared L2 or Euclidian norm of array `x`.

functions.name: pypret.lib.norm
documentation: Calculates the L2 or Euclidian norm of array `x`.

functions.name: pypret.lib.phase
documentation: The phase of a complex array.

functions.name: pypret.lib.nrms
documentation:
Calculates the normalized rms error between `x` and `y`.

The convention for normalization varies. Here we use:

```
max |y|
```

as normalization.

functions.name: pypret.lib.mean
documentation: Calculates the mean of the distribution described by (x, y).

functions.name: pypret.lib.variance
documentation: Calculates the variance of the distribution described by (x, y).

functions.name: pypret.lib.standard_deviation
documentation:
Calculates the standard deviation of the distribution described by
(x, y).

functions.name: pypret.lib.gaussian
documentation:
Calculates a Gaussian function with center `x0` and standard
deviation `sigma`.

functions.name: pypret.lib.rescale
documentation:
Rescales a numpy array to the range specified by `window`.

Default is [0, 1].

functions.name: pypret.lib.marginals
documentation:
Calculates the marginals of the data array.

axes specifies the axes of the marginals, e.g., the axes on which the
sum is projected.

If axis is None a list of all marginals is returned.

functions.name: pypret.lib.find
documentation: Return the index of the nth element that fulfills the condition.

functions.name: pypret.lib.best_scale
documentation:
Scales rho so that:

```
sum (rho * |E| - |E0|)^2
```

is minimal.

functions.name: pypret.lib.arglimit
documentation: Returns the first and last index where `y >= threshold * max(abs(y))`.

functions.name: pypret.lib.limit
documentation:
Returns the maximum x-range where the y-values are sufficiently large.

# Parameters

x

: The x values of the graph.

y

: The y values of the graph. If `None` the maximum range of `x` is
  used. That is only useful if `padding > 0`.

threshold

: The threshold relative to the maximum of `y` of values that should be
  included in the bracket.

padding

: The relative padding on each side in fractions of the bracket size.

extend

: Signals if the returned range can be larger than the values in `x`.
  Default is `True`.

# Returns

xl, xr

: Lowest and biggest value of the range.

functions.name: pypret.lib.fwhm
documentation:
Calculates the full width at half maximum of the distribution described
by (x, y).

functions.name: pypret.lib.edges
documentation:
Calculates the edges of the array elements.

Assuming that the input array contains the midpoints of a supposed data
set, the function returns the (N+1) edges of the data set points.

functions.name: pypret.lib.build_coords
documentation: Builds a coordinate array from the axes.

functions.name: pypret.lib.retrieval_report
documentation:
Simple helper that prints out important information from the
retrieval result object.

modules.name: pypret.random_pulse
documentation: Provides a function to generate random pulses with specified TBP.

functions.name: pypret.random_pulse.random_pulse
documentation:
Creates a random pulse with a specified time-bandwidth product.

Parameters
----------
pulse : Pulse instance
tbp : float
    The specified time-bandwidth product.
edge_value : float, optional
    The maximal value for the pulse amplitude at the edges of the grid.
    It defaults to the double value epsilon ~2e-16.

Returns
-------
bool : True on success, False if an error occured. The resulting pulse
    is stored in the Pulse instance passed to the function.

Notes
-----
The function creates random pulses by iteratively restricting the bandwidth
in time and frequency domain. It starts from random complex values in
frequency domain, multiplies a Gaussian function, transforms in the
time domain and multiplies a Gaussian function again. The filter functions
are Gaussians with the specified time-bandwidth product.
The TBP of the Gaussian filters, however, does not directly correspond
to the TBP of the resulting pulse. To use this algorithm to generate a
pulse with exactly the specified TBP, it is run in the range 0.5 * TBP to
1.5 * TBP using a scalar root search (brentq). Usually this guarantees
convergence within a few tries.
The larger the TBP the larger the number of points has to be. So the
algorithm may fail to find a solution if pulse.N is too small.

functions.name: pypret.random_pulse.random_gaussian
documentation:
Generates a Gaussian pulse with random phase.

Its pulse of duration is given by `fwhm`.

functions.name: pypret.autocorrelation.autocorrelation
documentation:
Calculates the intensity or second-order autocorrelation G2(tau).

# Parameters

pulse

: The pulse of which the autocorrelation is calculated

tau

: The delays at which the autocorrelation is evaluated. If `None`
  the temporal grid `pulse.t` of the pulse object is used.

collinear

: Calculates the collinear autocorrelation, with background and higher
  frequency terms. Otherwise only the non-collinear intensity
  autocorrelation without background is calculated. Default is `False`.

# Returns

tau

: The delay axis at which the autocorrelation was evaluated.

ac

: The autocorrelation signal.

# Notes

Calculates the following expression:

```
G2(tau) = int | [E(t-tau) + E(t)]^2 |^2 dt
```

where E(t) is the real-valued electric field.
This expression is expanded in terms of the complex-valued pulse envelope
and then evaluated with help of the convolution theorem and the Fourier
transform. Terms containing oscillations in t, e.g., exp(2j w0 t), are
neglected.
Specifically, it implements Eq. (9.7) on page 460 from [^cite_diels2006].

modules.name: pypret.fourier
documentation:
This module implements the Fourier transforms on linear grids.

The following code approximates the continuous Fourier transform (FT) on
equidistantly spaced grids. While this is usually associated with
'just doing a fast Fourier transform (FFT)', surprisingly, much can be done
wrong.

The reason is that the correct expressions depend on the grid location. In
fact, the FT can be calculated with one DFT but in general it requires a prior
and posterior multiplication with phase factors.

The FT convention we are going to use here is the following:

```
Ẽ(w) = 1/2pi ∫ E(t) exp(+i w t) dt
E(t) =       ∫ Ẽ(w) exp(-i t w) dw
```

where w is the angular frequency. We can approximate these integrals by their
Riemann sums on the following equidistantly spaced grids:

```
t_k = t_0 + k Δt, k=0, ..., N-1
w_n = w_0 + n Δw, n=0, ..., N-1
```

and define E_k = E(t_k) and Ẽ_n = Ẽ(w_n) to obtain:

```
Ẽ_n = Δt/2pi ∑_k E_k exp(+i w_n t_k)
E_k = Δw     ∑_n Ẽ_n exp(-i t_k w_n).
```

To evaluate the sum using the FFT we can expand the exponential to obtain:

```
Ẽ_n = Δt/2pi exp(+i n t_0 Δw) ∑_k [E_k exp(+i t_k w_0) ] exp(+i n k Δt Δw)
E_k = Δw     exp(-i t_k w_0)  ∑_n [Ẽ_n exp(-i n t_0 Δw)] exp(-i k n Δt Δw)
```

Additionally, we have to require the so-called reciprocity relation for
the grid spacings:

```
      !
Δt Δw = 2pi / N = ζ     (reciprocity relation)
```

This is what enables us to use the DFT/FFT! Now we look at the definition of
the FFT in NumPy:

```
 fft[x_m] -> X_k =     ∑_m exp(-2pi i m k / N)
ifft[X_k] -> x_m = 1/N ∑_k exp(+2pi i k m / N)
```

which gives the final expressions:

```
Ẽ_n = Δt N/2pi r_n   ifft[E_k s_k  ]
E_k = Δw       s_k^*  fft[Ẽ_n r_n^*]

with r_n = exp(+i n t_0 Δw)
     s_k = exp(+i t_k w_0)
```

where ^\* means complex conjugation. We see that the array to be transformed
has to be multiplied with an appropriate phase factor before and after
performing the DFT. And those phase factors mainly depend on the starting
points of the grids: w_0 and t_0. Note also that due to our sign convention
for the FT we have to use ifft for the forward transform and vice versa.

Trivially, we can see that for `w_0 = t_0 = 0` the phase factors vanish and
the FT is approximated well by just the DFT. However, in optics these
grids are unusual.
For `w_0 = l Δw` and `t_0 = m Δt`, where l, m are integers (i.e., w_0 and
t_0 are multiples of the grid spacing), the phase factors can be
incorperated into the DFT. Then the phase factors can be replaced by circular
shifts of the input and output arrays.

This is exactly what the functions (i)fftshift are doing for one specific
choice of l and m, namely for:

```
t_0 = -floor(N/2) Δt
w_0 = -floor(N/2) Δw.
```

In this specific case only we can approximate the FT by:

```
Ẽ_n = Δt N/2pi fftshift(ifft(ifftshift(E_k)))
E_k = Δw       fftshift( fft(ifftshift(Ẽ_n))) (no mistake!)
```

We see that the ifftshift \_always\_ has to appear on the inside. Failure to do
so will still be correct for even N (here fftshift is the same as ifftshift)
but will produce wrong results for odd N.

Additionally you have to watch out not to violate the assumptions for the
grid positions. Using a symmetrical grid, e.g.,:

```
x = linspace(-1, 1, 128)
```

will also produce wrong results, as the elements of x are not multiples of the
grid spacing (but shifted by half a grid point).

The main drawback of this approach is that circular shifts are usually far more
time- and memory-consuming than an elementwise multiplication, especially for
higher dimensions. In fact I see no advantage in using the shift approach at
all. But for some reason it got stuck in the minds of people and you find the
notion of having to re-order the output of the DFT everywhere.

Long story short: here we are going to stick with multiplying the correct
phase factors. The code tries to follow the notation used above.

Good, more comprehensive expositions of the issues above can be found in
[^cite_briggs1995] and [^cite_hansen2014]. For the reason why the first-order
approximation to the Riemann integral suffices, see [^cite_trefethen2014].

classes.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransformBase

methods.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransformBase.__init__
documentation:
Creates conjugate grids and calculates the Fourier transform.

# Parameters

N

: Array size

dt

: The temporal grid spacing. If `None` will be calculated by the
  reciprocity relation `dt = 2 * pi / (N * dw)`. Exactly one of
  `dt` or `dw` has be provided.

dw

: The spectral grid spacing. If `None` will be calculated by the
  reciprocity relation `dw = 2 * pi / (N * dt)`. Exactly one of
  `dt` or `dw` has be provided.

t0

: The first element of the temporal grid. If `None` will be
  `t0 = -floor(N/2) * dt`.

w0

: The first element of the spectral grid. If `None` will be
  `w0 = -floor(N/2) * dw`.

methods.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransformBase._post_init
documentation: Hook to initialize an object from storage.

methods.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransformBase.forward_at
documentation:
Calculates the forward Fourier transform of `x` at the
frequencies `w`.

This function calculates the Riemann sum directly and has quadratic
runtime. However, it can evaluate the integral at arbitrary
frequencies, even if they are non-equidistantly spaced. Effectively,
it performs a trigonometric interpolation.

methods.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransformBase.backward_at
documentation:
Calculates the backward Fourier transform of `x` at the
times `t`.

This function calculates the Riemann sum directly and has quadratic
runtime. However, it can evaluate the integral at arbitrary
times, even if they are non-equidistantly spaced. Effectively,
it performs a trigonometric interpolation.

This class implements the Fourier transform on linear grids.

This simple implementation is mainly for educational use.

Attributes
----------
N : int
    Size of the grid
dt : float
    Temporal spacing
dw : float
    Frequency spacing (angular frequency)
t0 : float
    The first element of the temporal grid
w0 : float
    The first element of the frequency grid
t : 1d-array
    The temporal grid
w : 1d-array
    The frequency grid (angular frequency)

methods.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransform.forward
documentation:
Calculates the (forward) Fourier transform of `x`.

For n-dimensional arrays it operates on the last axis, which has
to match the size of `x`.

# Parameters

x

: The array of which the Fourier transform will be calculated.

out

: A location into which the result is stored. If not provided or
  None, a freshly-allocated array is returned.

methods.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransform.backward
documentation:
Calculates the backward (inverse) Fourier transform of `x`.

For n-dimensional arrays it operates on the last axis, which has
to match the size of `x`.

# Parameters

x

: The array of which the Fourier transform will be calculated.

out

: A location into which the result is stored. If not provided or
  None, a freshly-allocated array is returned.

methods.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransform.forward
documentation:
Calculates the (forward) Fourier transform of `x`.

For n-dimensional arrays it operates on the last axis, which has
to match the size of `x`.

# Parameters

x

: The array of which the Fourier transform will be calculated.

out

: A location into which the result is stored. If not provided or
  None, a freshly-allocated array is returned.

methods.name: pypret.fourier.FourierTransform.backward
documentation:
Calculates the backward (inverse) Fourier transform of `x`.

For n-dimensional arrays it operates on the last axis, which has
to match the size of `x`.

# Parameters

x

: The array of which the Fourier transform will be calculated.

out

: A location into which the result is stored. If not provided or
  None, a freshly-allocated array is returned.

classes.name: pypret.fourier.Gaussian

methods.name: pypret.fourier.Gaussian.__init__
documentation:
Instantiates a shifted Gaussian function.

The Gaussian is calculated by:

```
f(t) = exp(-0.5 (t - t0)^2 / dt^2) * exp(1.0j * phase)
```

Its Fourier transform is:

```
F(w) = dt/sqrt(2pi) exp(-0.5 * (w + phase)^2 * dt^2 +
                         1j * t0 * w)
```

# Parameters

dt

: The standard deviation of the temporal amplitude distribution.

t0

: The center of the temporal amplitude distribution.

phase

: The linear phase coefficient of the temporal distribution.

methods.name: pypret.fourier.Gaussian.temporal
documentation: Returns the temporal distribution.

methods.name: pypret.fourier.Gaussian.spectral
documentation: Returns the spectral distribution.
This class can be used for testing the Fourier transform.

modules.name: pypret.frequencies
documentation:
This module handles conversion between frequency units.

The supported units and their shorthands are:

- wl : wavelength in meter
- om: angular frequency in rad/s
- f: frequency in 1/s
- k: angular wavenumber in rad/m

The conversion functions have the form `shorthand2shorthand` which is not
pythonic but very short. A more pythonic conversion can be achieved by using
the `convert` function

```pycon
>>> convert(x, 'wl', 'om')
```

The shorthands will be used throughout the package to identify frequency units.

The functions in this module should be used wherever a frequency convention
is necessary to avoid mistakes and make the code more expressive.

functions.name: pypret.frequencies.convert
documentation:
Convert between two frequency units.

Parameters
----------
x : float or array_like
    Numerical value or array that should be converted.
unit1, unit2 : str
    Shorthands for the original unit (`unit1`) and the destination unit
    (`unit2`).

Returns
-------
float or array_like
    The converted numerical value or array. It will always be a copy, even
    if `unit1 == unit2`.

Notes
-----
Unit shorthands can be any of
`wl` : wavelength in meter
`om` : angular frequency in rad/s
`f` : frequency in 1/s
`k` : angular wavenumber in rad/m
